title: Question: Verilog UDP to behavioral code conversion
body:
---


Author Name: **Dusan Grujic**
Original Redmine Message: 1669 from https://www.veripool.org



---

Verilog UDPs are commonly used in gate-level simulation libraries, which are needed for simulating the synthesized design.
However, Verilog UDPs currently can't be simulated with Verilator.
As a workaround, they can be translated to behavioral Verilog, which can be compiled with Verilator.
Details of the conversion, together with a small Python demonstration tool and example can be found at:
https://github.com/dgrujic/udp2beh

There are two types of Verilog UDPs - combinatorial and sequential, both represented by transition tables.
Combinatorial UDP conversion is trivial, since they can be replaced with casez statement.

Sequential UDP conversion is not so straight-forward.
Each table entry represents either a level- or edge-sensitive event which possibly changes the output.
UDP specification requires that all possible transitions, even the ones which are not changing the state of the output (dummy transitions), must be specified.
Any input not specified in table will result in output being set to X.
Probable reason for requiring specification of dummy transitions is to capture the illegal input combinations.
Since Verilator is working with 2 state logic, it cannot signal the illegal inputs by setting the output to X.
Hence the dummy transitions can be omitted entirely, and the table can be significantly simplified.

Each sequential UDP table entry is converted by making an event detector and output assignment Verilog blocks.
Event detector is a piece of Verilog code which detects the input combination for level-sensitive events, or edge for edge-sensitive events.
The template for level-sensitive event detector would be:
```
     reg event_n;
     always @(*)
     begin
         if ( inputs == table_entry)
             event_n = 1;
         else
             event_n = 0;
     end

```
where inputs and table_entry do not contain don't care bits.

The template for edge sensitive transitions event detector would be:
```
     reg event_n;
     always @(posedge edge_input or posedge event_n_clear)    //  or negedge edge_input, depending on table
     begin
         if ( event_n_clear)
             event_n = 0;
         else if ( inputs == table_entry)
             event_n = 1;
     end

```
where edge_input is the edge sensitive signal specified in table, and inputs and table_entry do not contain don't care bits and edge_input signal.

Output assignment should be performed in a single always @(*) block to avoid having multiple drivers for output.

Template output assignment block would be:
```
     always @(*)
     begin
         if (event_0)
             output = table_entry_0_output;
         .
         .
         .
         if (event_x)    // Edge events
         begin
             output = table_entry_x_output;
             event_x_clear = 1;
         end
         else
             event_x_clear = 0;        
         .
         .
         .
         if (event_n)
             output = table_entry_n_output
     end

```
Edge sensitive transitions are detected and set to 1 in event detector, and cleared by an assignment of event_x_clear = 1 in output assignment block.
The sequence of events is:
```
edge event occurs
event_n = 1 in edge-sensitive event detector
output is set to table defined value, event_n_clear = 1
event_n = 0
event_n_clear = 0

```
The described sequence of events is performed in simulator delta time.

The sequence of events for edge-sensitive transition is occurring in simulator delta time, and requires several evaluations.
This is not efficient for simulation, but I haven't found another way to implement edge-sensitive table entries in Verilog.
However, it might be more efficient if implemented directly in Verilator, since it (probably) can detect edge-sensitive events and set the output without the need for delta cycles.

I'm willing to try to implement this feature in Verilator, or provide help, but currently I can't get a grip on the code - i.e. I don't know where to start.
